title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about generation gaps and understanding between age groups. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Talking Across the Gap
content.subtitle: An article about intergenerational understanding and its challenges.
content.text:
Every generation believes, with complete sincerity, that the one following it is somehow softer, more confused, or less committed than its own. [31] This conviction is so consistent across cultures and centuries — documented in ancient Rome, in Renaissance Italy, and in every decade of modern sociology — that it begins to look less like an accurate observation and more like a reliable feature of the human tendency to read change as decline. [/31]

The current iteration of this anxiety centres, in much of the Western world, on the generation born roughly between 1997 and 2012, variously labelled Generation Z or 'zoomers'. Older commentators point to rising rates of anxiety and depression among young people, falling rates of risk-taking behaviours such as drinking and casual sex, and what they characterise as a fragility in the face of challenging ideas. [32] Younger commentators respond that what looks like fragility is in fact a heightened capacity to name and describe emotional experience — a skill their elders were never given the language for, and which they now mistake for weakness. [/32]

Both sides, it should be noted, are partially right. Longitudinal data on youth mental health does show a deterioration in wellbeing in many countries from the mid-2010s onwards, coinciding with the widespread adoption of social media. But the causes and meaning of this deterioration are fiercely debated. [33] Some researchers argue that the data reflects a genuine increase in distress; others suggest it may partly reflect an increased willingness to acknowledge and report difficulties that earlier generations experienced but did not name. [/33]

The difficulty of intergenerational understanding runs deeper than data disputes. Each generation grows up in a materially and culturally different world, and the experiences that shaped one cohort often cannot be straightforwardly communicated to another. [34] A person who came of age during a period of full employment and affordable housing will find it genuinely difficult to fully grasp the material constraints faced by young adults entering the current labour market. This is not a failure of empathy but a limit of imagination. [/34]

What tends to bridge these gaps is not argument but narrative. Sociologists who study intergenerational relations consistently find that shared personal stories — not statistics, not policy debates, but one person describing their experience to another — create the conditions for genuine understanding across age differences. [35] The grandparent who describes leaving school at fifteen and entering a factory does more to explain their worldview to a grandchild in a single conversation than any number of documentaries about postwar Britain. [/35]

There is also an asymmetry in intergenerational exchange that is rarely acknowledged: the young have always had to understand the old in order to function in a world built by previous generations, while the old have had the luxury of treating the young as a curiosity rather than a necessity. [36] As populations age and labour markets shift, this asymmetry is beginning to reverse, and organisations that have learned to genuinely listen across generational lines are finding that the exchange enriches everyone involved. [/36]

questions.question: The writer's reference to ancient Rome and Renaissance Italy is intended to show that
options: A) historians have always been interested in the behaviour of young people.
B) each generation's negative view of the next is a consistent feature of human behaviour, not a new insight.
C) the problems facing young people today are similar to those of previous centuries.
D) sociologists are more reliable observers of generational change than historians.
questions.explanation: The consistency across 'cultures and centuries' suggests this belief looks 'less like an accurate observation and more like a reliable feature of the human tendency' — it's a recurring pattern, not a new truth.

questions.question: Young people's response to being called fragile is that
options: A) the mental health data about their generation has been misrepresented.
B) older generations experienced the same problems but hid them.
C) they have a more developed vocabulary for describing emotional states than older people.
D) social media has been unfairly blamed for their psychological difficulties.
questions.explanation: Younger commentators say what looks like fragility is 'a heightened capacity to name and describe emotional experience — a skill their elders were never given the language for'.

questions.question: The debate among researchers about mental health data shows that
options: A) social media is definitely responsible for declining youth mental health.
B) official statistics about youth mental health cannot be trusted.
C) it is unclear whether the data represents more distress or simply more reporting of it.
D) young people in all countries are experiencing the same mental health trends.
questions.explanation: Some researchers see a 'genuine increase in distress'; others suggest it may reflect 'an increased willingness to acknowledge and report difficulties' — meaning the data's meaning is uncertain.

questions.question: When the writer says the difficulty older people have understanding young people's material constraints is 'not a failure of empathy but a limit of imagination', they mean
options: A) older people choose not to empathise with younger generations.
B) older people are unable to fully understand experiences so different from their own.
C) imagination is a more important quality than empathy for intergenerational understanding.
D) older people are unwilling to acknowledge the economic difficulties of the young.
questions.explanation: The phrase means older people do not lack the desire to understand (empathy) but are limited by their inability to fully imagine circumstances so different from their own experience.

questions.question: Sociologists studying intergenerational relations have found that understanding across age groups is best achieved through
options: A) sharing statistical information about different generations' experiences.
B) watching documentaries that explain the historical context of each generation.
C) personal stories told directly between individuals of different ages.
D) policy debates that focus on the needs of both old and young.
questions.explanation: Sociologists find that 'shared personal stories — not statistics, not policy debates, but one person describing their experience to another — create the conditions for genuine understanding'.

questions.question: The asymmetry the writer identifies in intergenerational exchange is that
options: A) young people communicate more effectively than older people.
B) older generations have typically expected to be understood rather than to understand.
C) organisations benefit more from listening to older employees than younger ones.
D) younger generations have more influence over social change than older ones.
questions.explanation: The young have always needed to understand the old to navigate an adult world, while the old 'have had the luxury of treating the young as a curiosity rather than a necessity' — not needing to understand them.
